messages.text: That sounds like a weirdly formal and emotionally distant relationship to have with your parents. (But my friend said that he always was harder on himself punishment wise than they would have been. Because they also had to assign themselves punishments and if they tried to go easy another incident report.) That doesn't make sense. If he had to assign the punishments to himself, but he couldn't assign himself an 'easy punishment', then that is the parents enforcing a harsh punishment. There's no actual choice there, just the illusion of it.

messages.speaker: A
messages.text: I honestly had so many questions but my friend swore it happened his entire childhood.  He said they told him, when he was older, that they thought it helped him reflect on his decisions and mistakes that led up to the “infractions!”   He actually has a close relationship with them and he is very cognizant of his actions, more than most.  But he said it was really the thing he dreaded.  He also said they never wanted to use physical violence or yelling so they came up with this.  I believe they even had to rate their actions on a scale of 1 -10.  He was a super nice guy and we joked that they were wardens or parole officer parents but the officer employee likeness is there too. I actually wanted to see the form he had to fill out because, yes, they had a printed form template the kids had to follow.

messages.speaker: B
messages.text: (He also said they never wanted to use physical violence or yelling so they came up with this.) Honestly, this actually sounds like it's way worse psychologically than being smacked or yelled at. If I had to choose between being smacked and going through this, I'd pick the former. It's not visibly violent but that doesn't mean it's less cruel. I'm not defending corporal punishment, I wasn't smacked myself growing up. But this sounds way more emotionally invasive. I'm glad your friend takes it in good spirits now, but I'm not a fan of the 'the ends justify the means' logic from the parents.
